= 0.277

Let's break down the first row of data. In the document, "The dog ran", the

word "The" appears 1 time in this doc and there are 3 words total making the

TF score 0.3333 repeated. There are 4 total docs and all 4 include the word

"The" making the IDF score zero because ln(4/4) = 0. Therefore, the TF-IDF

score is 0.3333 * 0 = 0. Again, the purpose of TF-IDF is to represent a

relative measure of how unique a word is across all documents and within the

document the score is being calculated against. As a result, the word "The"

has no importance because it appears in every document. That is no different

from a word that doesn't appear in a particular document at all. They both get

a TF-IDF score of zero. The best scores come from words that appear in

fewer documents in documents with fewer words.

Hopefully that makes a bit more sense and helps you understand what the

tfidf_matrix object holds that we created above. the next step is to calculate a

similarity matrix based on the TF-IDF scores. Just as with the collaborative

filtering model, there are many similarity scores that we can use here. For a

complete review, see 25.7. For this demonstration, we will stick with a

cosine-based similarity matrix just like that example. The code below creates

a matrix and prints it out in a temporary DataFrame just for viewing

purposes.

from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import linear_kernel

# Compute the cosine similarity between each movie description
cosine_sim = linear_kernel(tfidf_matrix, tfidf_matrix)


# For easier viewing, put it in a dataframe
pd.DataFrame(cosine_sim)

_Figure 26.2: Cosine Similarity Matrix for Netflix Descriptions_

To be clear, this is an 8803 by 8803 matrix that contains a cosine "similarity"

score representing how similar the movie/show descriptions are between

each pair of movies/shows. The cosine similarity score was calculated using

the vectors of TF-IDF scores in the prior step. Essentially, higher scores in

the matrix above indicate that the two movies/shows reprented by the row

and column ID numbers have descriptions with more of the same words. If

these movies/shows have useful descriptions that truly represent what the

content is about, then these cosine similarity scores will accurately indicate

which items to recommend based on an item of interest.

You may notice that the cosine similarity scores generated in this matrix are

different than those we calculated for the collaborative filtering example.

That is because we used the linear_kernel object from sklearn to compute

them. For a more thorough discussion of how the linear_kernel() object

calculates cosine similarity, you can read more here. But essentially, it


reverses the scale so that higher numbers mean "more similar" as opposed to

the opposite in the collaborative filtering example in the prior chapter where

a lower score represented a smaller angle and, thus, greater similarity. As a

result of how linear_kernel() calculates cosine similarity, the diagonal of that

table contains all 1.0000 values (the max possible) because that is the cosine

similarity of a movie description with itself.

This cosine_sim matrix object is basically the trained model we need to

make recommendations. For any movie/showID, we only need to rank sort

(descending) the cosine similarity scores and return the top n as

recommendations. In fact, let's try it to see if that works. The code below

sorts by column 0 descending and shows that movie 4877, 1066, 7503, and

5047 have the most similar descriptions to movie 0.

df_sorted = pd.DataFrame(cosine_sim).sort_values(by=[ 0 ], ascending=False)

for id in df_sorted.index[ 0 : 4 ]:
print(id, '\t', df.loc[id, 'title'])

display(df_sorted)

# Output:
# 0 Dick Johnson Is Dead
# 4877 End Game
# 1066 The Soul
# 7503 Moon
# 5047 The Cloverfield Paradox


_Figure 26.3: Cosine Similarity Matrix for Netflix Descriptions_

You may or may not recognize these shows, but if you read their descriptions,

you'll find that they truly do have the most similar text to movie ID 0, Dick

Johnson is Dead. Let's explore some good ways to deploy these

recommendations in the next section.

## 26.7Deployment

So how should we deploy this recommender model? As usual, let's make a

function that accepts a movie/item and returns the top n recommendations

for that item. To make this function as fast as possible, we won't put the

matrix in a DataFrame. Instead, it makes a list of the highest similarity-

ranked item indices and returns them in a python dictionary alongn with their

similarity scores.

def get_recommendations(item_id, sim_matrix, n= 10 , messages=True):
if not item_id in sim_matrix[:]: # Add some error checking for robustness
print(f&quot;Item {item_id} is not in the similarity matrix you provided&quot;)
return


# Get the pairwise similarity scores of all movies with that movie
sim_scores = list(enumerate(sim_matrix[item_id]))

# Sort the items based on the similarity scores
sim_scores = sorted(sim_scores, key=lambda x: x[ 1 ], reverse=True)

# Get the scores of the n most similar items; start at 1 so that it skips itself
top_similar = sim_scores[ 1 :n+ 1 ]

# Put the recommended item indices and similarity scores together in a dictionary
using comprehension
rec_dict = {i[ 0 ]:i[ 1 ] for i in top_similar}

if messages:
print(f&quot;The top recommended item IDs are: {list(rec_dict.keys())}&quot;)
print(f&quot;Their similarity scores are:\t {list(rec_dict.values())}&quot;)

# Return the top n most similar items
return rec_dict

Take a look through the code in this function. It requires an item_id to make

recommendations for. It also needs the similarity matrix. But it allows the

scores in the matrix to be calculated any way that you want to provide. It also

wants to know how many recommendations you need (n). Finally, it will print

out some details on how it processes the results if you want them.

Inside the function, it first checks to make sure you've provided a valid

item_id. Then, it selects all of the similarity scores for that item_id. Then, it

sorts the scores descending so the best scores are first. Then, it selects only

the top n from that list and adds the recommended item_ids and their

similarity scores to a dictionary using comprehension. It will print out the

details of that dictionary if unless you tell the function not to

(messages=False). Finally, it returns the results.

Now that we have the function, let's practice calling it. We'll add the results

to a DataFrame that we join with the original df DataFrame so that we can

view the title, cast, release_year, rating, and listed_in (i.e. genres) along with

the similarity scores.


# Change this value to any title you'd like to get recommendations
title = &quot;Dick Johnson Is Dead&quot;

# Check if the title is valid; if not, suggest alternatives and use the last one for
recommendations
if title in df['title'].to_list():
id = df.index[df['title']==title][ 0 ] # Convert the title to an index (i.e. item
ID)
else:
print(f&quot;\&quot;{title}\&quot; is not in the data set. Try one of
these:\n&quot;)
for row in df.sample(n= 10 ).itertuples(): # Get a random 10 titles
id = row[ 0 ]
title = row.title
print(f'\t{title}')

print(f&quot;\nIf you like \&quot;{title},\&quot; then you may also like:\n&quot;)

# Call the function and return the dictionary; print out the dictionary if you want
to see what it is
recommend_dict = get_recommendations(id, cosine_sim, n= 10 , messages=False)

# Add the dictionary to a new DataFrame; this isn't necessary, but it helps to see
what movies are recommended
df_similarity = pd.DataFrame(data=recommend_dict.values(), columns=['similarity'],
index=recommend_dict.keys())

# Create a subset of the original df DataFrame with only the recommended movies
df_recommendations = df.loc[df.index.isin(recommend_dict.keys()), ['title', 'cast',
'release_year', 'rating', 'listed_in']]

# Join the original df results with the recommended movie similarity scores so that
we can sort the list and view it
df_recommendations.join(df_similarity).sort_values(by=['similarity'],
ascending=False)

Again, we didn't really need to view the results in a DataFrame along with

the similarity scores in order for the function to work. We just did that as a

sanity check to see if the results looked valid. The function only returns a

dictionary of movie IDs and similarity scores which is all we would need to

deploy this model in an app or website.


You may find it useful to try this content-filtering recommender model with

some other titles. You can print out the entire list from the df DataFrame and

copy/paste any movie title into the function all above. Here is a sample list

you can try.

Solo: A Star Wars Story

Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse

The Blue Planet: A Natural History of the Oceans

The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King

The Time Traveler's Wife

Zombieland

The Boss Baby: Get That Baby!

PJ Masks

The Karate Kid

My Little Pony: Friendship Is Magic

However, you'll notice that I wrote the code above to allow you to specify an

incorrect title name. If you do, then it will randomly select 10 valid titles or

you to choose from.

So how should you use this content-filtering recommender algorithm in an

app or website? You have the same two general options that were introduced

to you in the prior chapter in 25.8. I think Option 2 from that chapter is the

simplest; meaning that you run your Jupyter Notebook file to include

exporting the recommendation results for every item in the database that

your app or website uses on a regular schedule so that the recommendations

are constantly updated to reflect the lastest list of titles. How regular? Well,

since content filtering doesn't depend on user ratings, you would only have to

update the recommendations any time the database list of items is updated.

So, if Netflix updates their catalog monthly, then you would need to rerun

this code once a month just after the titles list is updated.

## 26.8Practice


Try working through these practice problems below.

Practice #1: Create a pipeline for content filtering.

As you know, functions create reusable code that can save us time and enable

smooth pipelines. You already have a function from this chapter for making

content filtering-based recommendations. For this practice, you will create

functions for all remaining steps in the pipeline.

First, create a function that performs the same cleaning steps that we

performed in the chapter for the netflix_titles.csv dataset. This function does

not need to be particularly dynamic. It should just return a dataset with no

missing values that has had the index reset so that all movie IDs are

consecutive in the index.

Next, create a function that calculates TF-IDF scores and generates the

similarity matrix. It should require the DataFrame that was cleaned from the

prior function and also allow, as a parameter, the name of the feature that has

unstructured text that will be used to calculate TF-IDF scores in the matrix.

Call this function tfidf_matrix().

Then, either use the existing function from the chapter called

get_recommendations() or create a new one to accomplish the same purpose.

It should accept an item_id, the similarity matrix, and the number of

recommendations you want returned. It should return the recommended

movie IDs (not titles) in the order of similarity as well as the similarity

scores. Again, you could just copy the function from the chapter.

Then, create a method (not a function because it won't have a return

statement) that allows you to pass in a movie title and anything else you need

to make this method work well. This method will print out the n titles

recommended for the submitted title. In other words, this function will call


get_recommendations() to return movie IDs, but then it will convert them

into titles. If you enter an invalid title, it should inform you that it was

invalid and it should then suggest 10 random other titles to try instead. This

function should print out the following text, "If you liked [movie name]

staring [cast], then you may like these other movies including a similar cast:"

Then it should print a DataFrame of the recommended movie titles, casts,

and the similarity score.

Finally call these functions in the proper order using the netflix_titles.csv

dataset once again. But use the "cast" feature instead of the "description"

feature to calculate the similarity scores. Get 5 recommendations for the

movie, "The Polar Express". Try spelling the movie title incorrectly to see if

your logic works in the last method.

Click the Colab icon to the right to see one possible solution to this practice

problem.

Practice #2: Extending Similarity

Using the same Netflix dataset as found in the chapter, and the pipeline you

created in the prior practice problem, expand the content filtering to include

all of the following features together: type, title, director, cast, country,

listed_in, and description. One way to do this is to merge/concatenate each of

those string features into a single column and base your model on that

combined column. However, be sure to clean the data first by addressing all

missing data.

There are many ways to accomplish this task using the pipeline you have

already created in Practice #1. Feel free to modify any of the prior functions

to make this work.


Click the Colab icon to the right to see one possible solution to this practice

problem.

## 27.1Introduction

We have covered a lot of ground in this course. There are several ways you

can show off what you have learned. This chapter contains three final

exams and projects. Your instructor will likely require only one or two of

the three options in this chapter. Speak to your instructor for further

instructions.


## 27.2Part A: Final Project

So you are ready to begin working on the final project? Your first step is to

find a dataset that is interesting to you. However, to demonstrate your

ability to collect data from the web, you need to create either a web

scraping routing or a web service API request. In addition, you need to

collect five types of data: numbers (either continuous or integer),

standardized categorical groups (meaning the same values are repeated),

unstructured text (e.g., tweets, product reviews, descriptions), images, and

one or more labels among those numeric and categorical features. The

complete details are found below:

## Supplement: Technical Interview Preparation


For each interview question below, identify which of the four signal types it

primarily evaluates: (a) Foundational knowledge, (b) Applied problem

solving, (c) Communication, or (d) Engineering and operational judgment.

1. “Explain the bias–variance tradeoff to a product manager who has no

statistics background.”

2. “Write a SQL query that finds the top 5 customers by lifetime value

using window functions.”

3. “Your deployed model’s accuracy dropped 8% last month. Walk me

through how you would investigate.”

4. “What is the difference between L1 and L2 regularization? When

would you choose each?”

5. “Design a data pipeline to retrain a recommendation model weekly

with fresh user interaction data.”

6. “You are given a dataset you have never seen before and asked to

predict customer churn. Walk me through your approach from start to

finish.”

7. “Tell me about a time you had to present analytical results to

stakeholders who disagreed with your findings.”

8. “How would you monitor a credit scoring model for fairness across

demographic groups after deployment?”

9. “Derive the gradient update rule for logistic regression.”

10. “A stakeholder asks why your model rejected a specific loan

application. How would you explain the decision?”

Answer Key

**Practice: Signal Type Classification**


1. (c) Communication — explaining a concept to a non-technical audience

2. (a) Foundational knowledge — tests SQL fluency

3. (d) Engineering judgment — production debugging and operational reasoning

4. (a) Foundational knowledge — concept recall and comparison

5. (d) Engineering judgment — system design for production infrastructure

6. (b) Applied problem solving — open-ended analytical process question

7. (c) Communication — behavioral question testing stakeholder communication

8. (d) Engineering judgment — post-deployment monitoring and fairness

9. (a) Foundational knowledge — mathematical derivation

10. (c) Communication — model explainability for a non-technical audience

Interviewers evaluate STAR responses for completeness, specificity, and

impact. For each response excerpt below, identify the primary weakness.

1. “We had a really messy dataset, so the team cleaned it up and built a

model. It worked well and the stakeholders were happy.” What is the

primary weakness?

(a) Missing Situation — no context about the project or

organization

(b) Missing Task — what was the candidate’s specific role?

(c) Missing Action — no specifics about what was done or how

(d) All of the above — every STAR component is vague or missing

2. “I was the lead analyst on a customer segmentation project for a retail

client with $50M annual revenue. I needed to identify high-value

customer segments to target for a retention campaign. I used K-means

clustering on RFM features. The clusters performed well.” What is the

primary weakness?

**Practice: Evaluating STAR Responses**


(a) Missing Situation

(b) Missing Task

(c) Missing Action detail

(d) Missing Result — no measurable outcome or business impact

3. “In my capstone project, our team of four needed to predict loan

defaults. I personally built the feature engineering pipeline, handling

missing values with KNN imputation and creating 12 interaction

features. I also set up the cross-validation framework. Our model

achieved 0.87 AUC on the holdout set, which was the highest in the

class.” This response is:

(a) Weak — too much technical detail

(b) Strong — it specifies the candidate’s personal contribution,

gives technical specifics, and quantifies the result

(c) Weak — it does not mention the team enough

(d) Weak — it should not mention being the highest in the class

4. “When the model failed in production, I felt terrible and spent the

whole weekend debugging. I eventually found the problem and fixed

it.” What is the primary weakness?

(a) Too emotional

(b) Missing specifics — what was the failure, what debugging steps

were taken, and what was the fix?

(c) Too short

(d) Should not mention weekend work

5. “I disagreed with my manager about using a neural network. I believed

a gradient boosted tree would be more interpretable for the compliance

team. I presented a comparison showing the GBT achieved within 1%

AUC of the neural net while providing feature importances the


compliance team could audit. We went with the GBT and passed the

regulatory review on the first attempt.” This response demonstrates:

(a) Insubordination

(b) Technical leadership — data-driven pushback, clear tradeoff

reasoning, and a measurable positive outcome

(c) Lack of flexibility

(d) Bias against neural networks

Answer Key

1. (d) Every component is vague

2. (d) Missing Result — good S, T, A but no measurable impact

3. (b) Strong response — clear personal contribution, technical specifics, quantified

outcome

4. (b) Missing specifics — the response needs concrete debugging steps and the fix

5. (b) Technical leadership — respectful pushback backed by evidence with a positive

outcome

<{http://www.bookeducator.com/Textbook}learningobjective >Students will

be able to identify the four signal types (foundational knowledge, applied

problem solving, communication, and engineering judgment) that technical

interviews evaluate and tailor preparation accordingly

<{http://www.bookeducator.com/Textbook}learningobjective >Students will

be able to construct complete STAR-format behavioral interview responses

that include measurable outcomes and connect technical work to business

impact <{http://www.bookeducator.com/Textbook}learningobjective

>Students will be able to explain core data science and ML concepts at an

interview level, including algorithm mechanics, evaluation metrics, feature

selection, and deployment considerations

<{http://www.bookeducator.com/Textbook}learningobjective >Students will

be able to design and execute a structured interview preparation plan using

deliberate practice principles targeted to their specific career track

<{http://www.bookeducator.com/Textbook}learningobjective >Students will


be able to transform coursework projects into interview-ready portfolio

pieces with business framing, quantified results, and pipeline-level context

## 28.1How to Use This Chapter

Image Description

Technical interviews for data-centric roles require a mix of solid

fundamentals, practical problem-solving, and knowledge of what each

company values. The demand for these skills is substantial: the U.S. Bureau

of Labor Statistics projects that data scientist employment will grow 36%

from 2023 to 2033, far outpacing the average for all occupations (U.S.

Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2024). LinkedIn’s 2025 Skills on the Rise report

identifies AI literacy and machine learning as among the fastest-growing

professional skills globally (LinkedIn, 2025). This chapter provides a

structured, evidence-based guide to preparing for interviews across three

**Choose Your Track**


career tracks: Data Analyst / Business Intelligence, Data Scientist, and ML

Engineer / MLOps. The advice here is grounded in publicly available

guidance from major employers (Google, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft),

established career platforms, and research on effective skill acquisition.

Not every section applies equally to every role. Use the table below to

prioritize your preparation based on the track that best matches your target

position. All three tracks share foundational elements—communication,

behavioral interviewing, and core technical literacy—but differ in depth and

emphasis.

Study Plans (Sec. 8) Essential Essential Essential

**Data Analyst / BI track.** Prioritize SQL, metrics interpretation, and

communication drills. Many companies hire analysts primarily on the ability

to retrieve, validate, and explain data rather than build or deploy models

(Coursera, 2025). Pipeline depth from later chapters is helpful context but

not typically required for analyst-level interviews.

**Data Scientist track.** Prioritize modeling, evaluation, experimentation, and

feature engineering. Add deployment literacy so you can explain how your

work ships to production. As Chip Huyen (2022) notes, modern data science

interviews increasingly test system-level thinking alongside algorithmic

knowledge.

**ML Engineer / MLOps track.** Prioritize end-to-end pipeline design,

reliability, monitoring, and retraining. Treat deployment and monitoring as

first-class skills rather than supplementary topics. AWS hiring managers

emphasize that ML engineering interviews focus heavily on building reliable,

scalable systems that maintain performance over time (Amazon Web

Services, 2024).

All of the content in this chapter has been informed and verified by industry

experts, hiring managers, and published research. The references below

represent the sources used throughout this chapter. Students are encouraged

to explore these resources to deepen their understanding and to see the

evidence behind each recommendation firsthand.

## 28.2What Interviews Actually Measure

Before diving into technical preparation, it helps to understand what

interviewers are actually trying to learn about you. Interview formats vary

across companies and roles, but the underlying signal types are remarkably

consistent.

Many organizations use _structured interviews_ —consistent questions with

scoring rubrics—because research shows they are more predictive of job

performance than unstructured conversations. As Laszlo Bock, former SVP

of People Operations at Google, explains in _Work Rules!_ , structured

interviews reduce bias and produce more defensible hiring decisions (Bock,

2015). This is why behavioral questions often feel scripted: they are designed

to be comparable across candidates.

Across companies and roles, interviewers evaluate four recurring signal

types:

**Why the Format Varies—and What Stays Constant**


1. **Foundational knowledge.** Can you demonstrate command of the

technical concepts required for the role? For analysts, this means SQL

fluency and statistical reasoning. For data scientists, it includes

modeling algorithms and evaluation metrics. For ML engineers, it

extends to system design and infrastructure. Google structures its

evaluation around “Role-Related Knowledge” as one of four core

hiring attributes, testing whether candidates have the technical depth

their specific role demands (Google Careers, 2024).

2. **Applied problem solving.** Can you break down an unfamiliar problem,

identify an approach, and execute under constraints? Google calls this

“General Cognitive Ability” and values it as highly as domain

expertise, noting that in open-ended problems, “your ability to explain

your thought process and how you use data to inform decisions is what

is most important” (Google Careers, 2024). Amazon tests this through

deep technical dives where interviewers probe with “what if”

variations and edge cases to see how candidates handle increasing

complexity (Amazon, 2024).

3. **Communication.** Can you explain technical work clearly to different

audiences? Meta explicitly evaluates whether candidates can “reason

from first principles, use metrics to drive decisions, and communicate

clearly while challenging assumptions” (Meta Careers, 2024).

Microsoft assesses candidates’ ability to translate technical findings

into stakeholder-ready language (Microsoft Careers, 2024). In practice,

this means you should be able to explain a model’s output to a product

manager just as clearly as you explain the algorithm to a fellow

engineer.

4. **Engineering and operational judgment.** Can you reason about what

happens after the model is built? This is especially important for DS

and ML roles that touch production systems. Interviewers probe for


awareness of training/serving skew, feedback loops, drift, and

monitoring—the real-world operational patterns that Zinkevich (n.d.)

codifies as “Rules of Machine Learning.” Even for analyst roles,

showing awareness of data quality and pipeline reliability signals

maturity.

The weight placed on each signal varies by role. The table below shows

typical emphasis patterns:

Table 28.2

Signal Emphasis by Role

**Signal Type**

**Data Analyst /**

**BI**

**Data Scientist**

**ML Engineer**

**/ MLOps**

Foundational

Knowledge

SQL, statistics,

metrics

Algorithms,

evaluation,

experimentation

Systems,

infrastructure,

algorithms

Applied

Problem

Solving

Case questions,

metric

investigation

Modeling

problems, tradeoff

analysis

System design,

scaling scenarios

Communication High emphasis High emphasis Moderate

emphasis

Engineering

Judgment

Low–moderate Moderate–high High

Understanding these signals helps you prepare strategically. Rather than

studying everything equally, focus your energy on the signals most heavily

weighted for your target role. Google’s own interview preparation guide

reinforces this point, advising candidates to focus on the specific skills and

knowledge areas highlighted in the job description rather than trying to

prepare for everything at once (Google Careers, 2024b). The rest of this

chapter is organized to help you do exactly that.


Most data-centric interview processes include some combination of the

following stages:

**Recruiter screen.** A 20–30 minute call to verify basic qualifications,

discuss the role, and assess mutual interest. Prepare a concise summary

of your background and be ready to explain why you are interested in the

specific company and role. As one LinkedIn career guide advises, “They

reached out for a reason—you’re qualified,” so use this stage to learn

about the process rather than prove yourself (Anagu, 2025).

**Technical screen.** A 45–60 minute session focused on core technical

skills—typically SQL, Python/R coding, or statistics questions. This is

often conducted via a shared coding environment. Practice writing syntax

without an IDE, as Meta and other companies conduct these screens on

shared documents or whiteboards (Interview Query, 2025).

**Take-home assignment.** A multi-hour project completed on your own

time, typically involving data cleaning, analysis, modeling, and a written

summary. Common for analyst and DS roles. See Section 6 for detailed

preparation guidance.

**Onsite / virtual loop.** Multiple back-to-back interviews (typically 3–5

sessions) covering different evaluation areas. Amazon dedicates

approximately half of the interview loop for technical roles to technical

assessments (coding, system design) and the other half to behavioral and

leadership questions (Amazon, 2024). Google and Microsoft use similar

multi-round structures.

**Presentation or case study.** Some companies (especially for senior or

research-oriented roles) ask candidates to present past work or solve a

case study in front of a panel. Prepare to discuss projects in depth: what

**Common Interview Formats**


problem you solved, which data you used, what tradeoffs you navigated,

and what impact your work had.

Research the Specific Process

Always research the interview process for your specific company and role

before preparing. As Coursera’s interview guide recommends, “the best place

to start is by researching the role you are applying for, as well as the

company itself”—use the job description as a preparation checklist and look

for interview reports on Glassdoor to learn what recent candidates

experienced (Coursera, 2025; Glassdoor, 2024).

## 28.3Preparation Strategy

The single most important insight about interview preparation is that

_deliberate practice_ outperforms unstructured repetition. Research on expert

performance consistently shows that targeted practice—focusing on a narrow

skill, drilling it with feedback, and reflecting on errors—produces faster

improvement than simply doing lots of problems without reflection (Young

et al., 2021). Apply this principle to your interview preparation by structuring

your study around short, focused sessions rather than long, unfocused cram

blocks.

A practical deliberate practice loop for interview preparation has four steps:

1. **Select a narrow skill.** Choose one specific area (e.g., window

functions in SQL, cross-validation mechanics, STAR-format

storytelling) rather than “general data science.”

**How to Study Efficiently**


2. **Drill with feedback.** Work through 3–5 practice problems, then check

your answers against a reference or explain your solution to someone

else. If practicing alone, record yourself answering and play it back.

Google’s free Interview Warmup tool provides AI-powered feedback

on spoken or typed answers across multiple categories including data

analytics, offering a structured way to practice without a partner

(Google, 2025).

3. **Identify the gap.** After each drill, ask: What did I struggle with?

Where did my explanation break down? What concept did I reach for

but could not recall?

4. **Target the gap in the next session.** Use the identified weakness as the

focus of your next practice session. This creates a virtuous cycle of

targeted improvement.

Structure your overall preparation around three recurring interview tasks that

appear across nearly all data-centric roles:

1. **Explain a concept clearly (teach-back).** Practice explaining technical

concepts as if teaching a colleague who is smart but unfamiliar with

the specific topic. This tests both your understanding and your

communication skill. Google interviewers emphasize that explaining

your logic and checking assumptions as you go demonstrates

communication and collaboration skills alongside technical acumen

(Google Careers, 2024).

2. **Solve a constrained problem.** Practice SQL queries, coding exercises,

and modeling problems under time pressure. Use a timer and a blank

editor (no autocomplete, no documentation) to simulate interview

conditions. As Indeed’s career guide suggests, timing yourself on


sample questions builds the confidence needed to perform under

pressure (Indeed, 2024).

3. **Justify tradeoffs.** Practice articulating why you would choose one

approach over another—why this metric instead of that one, why this

algorithm instead of an alternative, what risks a deployment choice

introduces. This is the signal type that distinguishes experienced

practitioners from candidates who can only follow recipes.

One of the most valuable assets you can bring to an interview is a portfolio of

projects that demonstrate end-to-end analytical thinking. Kaggle’s annual

survey of data professionals consistently shows that project portfolios and

competition experience are among the most effective signals for hiring

managers evaluating entry-level candidates (Kaggle, 2024). The good news is

that this book provides the raw material: every chapter exercise, case study,

and assignment is a potential portfolio piece.

To transform coursework into interview-ready projects, follow this

framework:

1. **Frame the problem in business terms.** Do not describe your project

as “I built a random forest model.” Instead, frame it as “I predicted

customer churn to help a subscription business prioritize retention

interventions.” Interviewers want to see that you connect technical

work to business value.

2. **Quantify your results.** Amazon’s interview guidance explicitly

encourages candidates to include metrics in their stories and to spell

out their personal contributions (Amazon, 2024). Instead of “the model

**Building Your Project Portfolio from Coursework**


worked well,” say “the model achieved 0.87 AUC on held-out data, a

12-point improvement over the baseline logistic regression.”

3. **Anticipate follow-up questions.** For every project you plan to discuss,

prepare answers for: Why did you choose that algorithm? What

alternatives did you consider? How did you handle missing data? What

would you do differently? How would you deploy this? These are

exactly the “dive deep” questions that Amazon and Google

interviewers use to probe beyond surface-level knowledge.

4. **Show pipeline thinking.** Describe your project as a sequence of

connected decisions (problem framing → data acquisition →

preparation → modeling → evaluation), not just a modeling exercise.

This demonstrates the end-to-end mindset that this book develops

throughout Chapters 1–18.

Start Now, Not Later

Do not wait until you are actively job-searching to prepare. Keep a running

document of project summaries as you complete chapter exercises. When

interview season arrives, you will have a library of stories rather than a blank

page. Career advisors consistently find that “80% of interview success is

preparation and strategy” (Anagu, 2025)—the students who prepare early

have a significant advantage.

## 28.4Behavioral Interviewing

Behavioral questions appear in every interview loop, regardless of role or

company. They exist because past behavior is one of the best predictors of

future performance, and structured behavioral interviews are more reliable

**How to Answer in a Way Interviewers Can Score**


than unstructured conversations (Bock, 2015). Understanding how these

questions work—and how they are scored—gives you a meaningful

advantage.

The most effective framework for behavioral answers is the **STAR method** :

_Situation, Task, Action, Result_. This structure produces complete, scorable

answers because it provides context, decisions, and outcomes in a predictable

sequence (U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, n.d.).

Table 28.3

STAR Method Components

**Component What to Include Common Mistakes**

**Situation** Set the scene: what was the

context? What team,

project, or organization?

What constraints existed?

Too vague (“at my last job”)

or too long (spending 3

minutes on background

before reaching the point)

**Task** What were you specifically

responsible for? What was

the goal or challenge?

Describing the team’s task

without clarifying your

personal role

**Action** What did you do? What

decisions did you make and

why? What tradeoffs did

you navigate?

Saying “we” instead of “I”;

listing activities without

explaining reasoning

**Result** What happened? Quantify

outcomes where possible.

What did you learn?

Omitting results entirely or

giving vague outcomes (“it

went well”)

Amazon’s interview guidance is particularly explicit about the importance of

structured behavioral answers. Every Amazon interview question maps to

one or more of their 16 Leadership Principles, and interviewers score

candidates against specific rubrics. Amazon advises candidates to “prepare

specific examples using the STAR format that highlight your impact,

complete with context, actions, and results” (Amazon, 2024). Even if you are


not interviewing at Amazon, this discipline produces clearer, more

memorable answers at any company.

Key principles for strong behavioral answers:

**Emphasize measurable outcomes and constraints.** Interviewers score

more cleanly when you name tradeoffs and results rather than just

activities. Instead of “I cleaned the data and built a model,” say “I

identified that 30% of records had missing values in three key features,

implemented KNN imputation which preserved 95% of the dataset, and

the final model reduced prediction error by 18% compared to the

previous quarter’s approach.”

**Prepare 6–8 stories that cover common themes.** Most behavioral

questions map to a small set of themes: teamwork and conflict, ambiguity

and initiative, failure and learning, technical leadership, and time

pressure. Prepare stories from your coursework, projects, internships, or

work experience that address each theme. You can adapt the same story to

multiple questions by emphasizing different aspects.

**Practice saying your stories out loud.** Written preparation is necessary

but not sufficient. Practice delivering your STAR stories verbally until

they feel natural and take 60–90 seconds each. Google interviewers note

that candidates who talk through their answers methodically, rather than

jumping to conclusions, demonstrate the structured thinking the company

values (Google Careers, 2024).

**Connect technical stories to business impact.** For data-centric roles,

your best behavioral stories often involve technical work. When telling

these stories, always close the loop on impact: How did the analysis

influence a decision? How did the model change a process? What

business metric improved? Meta explicitly evaluates whether candidates


can tie technical decisions back to user experience and business outcomes

(Meta Careers, 2024).

## 28.5The Technical Core

This section maps interview preparation directly to the chapters and skills

covered in this book. For each pipeline stage, we describe what you should be

ready to do, what decision implications to practice articulating, and how

major employers evaluate these skills.

Hands-On Modeling Practice

**Download:** interview_prep_modeling_scenarios.ipynb — This Jupyter

notebook presents 5 realistic interview scenarios using the Customer Churn

dataset. Scenarios cover stratified splitting, metric selection for business

objectives, data leakage detection, fair cross-validated model comparison,

and confusion matrix interpretation. Each scenario includes automated

verification checks.

Data work questions test your ability to acquire, clean, explore, and prepare

data—the stages that consume the majority of effort in real analytical

projects. These questions appear in virtually every interview loop for every

data-centric role. LinkedIn’s analysis of job postings shows that SQL,

Python, and data wrangling consistently rank among the most requested

skills for analytics and data science positions (LinkedIn, 2025).

**Mapped to This Book**

## Data Work (Chapters 2–8)

**What You Should Be Ready to Do**


**Diagnose messy data quickly.** Given a dataset description, explain how

you would identify and handle missing values, outliers, duplicates, and

inconsistent formats. Describe a repeatable cleaning strategy, not an ad-

hoc fix. This reflects the operational reality that data preparation

dominates effort in ML systems (Chapters 2–3, 7).

**Write SQL fluently.** Be prepared to write queries involving joins,

aggregations, window functions, subqueries, and CTEs on a whiteboard or

shared editor. SQL proficiency is “an essential skill for data science roles,

regardless of the company” (Coursera, 2025). Practice without

autocomplete or documentation (Chapter 4).

**Explain what makes a pipeline reliable.** Describe consistent

transformations, validation checks, and clear handoffs from raw data

through preparation to modeling. Show that you understand the difference

between one-off notebook analysis and reproducible, automated

workflows (Chapters 7–8).

**Explore data systematically.** Describe how you would profile a new

dataset: check distributions, identify data types, assess cardinality, detect

relationships, and surface anomalies before modeling. Interviewers want

to see that you look before you model (Chapters 6, 8).

Interviewers reward candidates who articulate _why_ they make choices, not

just _what_ they do. Practice saying these kinds of statements naturally:

“I separate data definition problems from model problems because

monitoring and governance depend on knowing which stage failed.”

“I would check for missingness patterns before choosing an imputation

strategy, because MCAR, MAR, and MNAR require different

**Decision Implications to Practice Saying Out Loud**


approaches.”

“For this use case I would normalize the features because the distance-

based algorithm is sensitive to scale, but I would document the

transformation so it can be inverted for interpretation.”

Data leakage is one of the most common pitfalls tested in technical

interviews. For each scenario, identify whether leakage is present and, if so,

what type.

1. A model predicts whether a patient will be readmitted within 30 days.

One feature is “number of follow-up appointments scheduled.” Is there

leakage?

(a) No — follow-up appointments are routine clinical data

(b) Yes — follow-up appointments are often scheduled because the

patient was readmitted, making this a target leakage feature

(c) No — because the feature was collected before the 30-day

window

(d) Yes — but only if the feature has high correlation with the

target

2. A student fits a StandardScaler on the entire dataset (train + test)

before splitting. The test ROC AUC is 0.93, but the model scores 0.86

on new data. What happened?

(a) The model needs more epochs

(b) Test set statistics leaked into the scaler, inflating test

performance

(c) The new data has different features

**Practice: Leakage Detection**


(d) The scaler is unnecessary for this model type

3. A churn model uses “account_status” as a feature, where one of the

values is “closed.” The model achieves 99% accuracy. What is the

problem?

(a) The feature is too noisy

(b) The feature directly encodes the target — closed accounts are

churned customers

(c) The model is overfitting

(d) The sample size is too small

4. A team performs feature selection using SelectKBest on the full

dataset, then splits into train/test. Is this leakage?

(a) No — feature selection is a preprocessing step

(b) Yes — feature selection used the test set’s target values to

choose features, biasing the result

(c) No — as long as the model is fit only on the training set

(d) It depends on the selection method

5. A time-series forecasting model uses data from January through

December. The team randomly splits the data into 80/20 train/test. Is

there a problem?

(a) No — random splitting is always appropriate

(b) Yes — random splitting allows future data to leak into training;

temporal data should use a time-based split

(c) No — as long as you use cross-validation

(d) Yes — but only if the time series has seasonality

6. A model predicts employee attrition. Features include

“exit_survey_score.” What is the concern?


(a) No concern — survey data is standard HR data

(b) Exit surveys are completed only by employees who are leaving,

so this feature is only available after the target event has occurred

(c) The feature is too subjective

(d) Survey scores are not numeric enough for modeling

Answer Key

1. (b) Target leakage — follow-ups are often a consequence of readmission

2. (b) Scaler leak — test set mean/std influenced the transformation

3. (b) Direct target encoding — “closed” status is the target in disguise

4. (b) Feature selection leak — test targets influenced which features were kept

5. (b) Temporal leakage — future observations can appear in training folds

6. (b) Post-event feature — exit surveys only exist for employees who already left

Modeling and evaluation questions are the core of data science interviews

and appear frequently in ML engineering interviews as well. As Huyen

(2022) emphasizes, modern ML interviews increasingly test not just whether

you can implement an algorithm, but whether you can reason about its

assumptions, failure modes, and operational implications. These questions

test your understanding of algorithms, your ability to evaluate model

performance rigorously, and your judgment about when and why to choose

one approach over another.

## Modeling and Evaluation (Chapters 9–15)

**What You Should Be Ready to Do**


**Explain core algorithms at an interview level.** Be prepared to describe

how linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, random forests,

gradient boosting, and basic neural networks work—including their

assumptions, strengths, and limitations. You do not need to derive every

equation, but you should be able to explain the intuition and key

mechanics clearly (Chapters 9, 12–14).

**Explain evaluation choices.** Describe why you would use a train/test

split versus cross-validation, when accuracy is an appropriate metric

versus precision/recall/F1 or AUC, and how to set classification

thresholds based on business costs. Cross-validation is a core concept for

robust model assessment, and scikit-learn’s documentation explicitly

warns against common evaluation pitfalls like data leakage through

improper validation (Scikit-learn Developers, n.d.). (Chapter 15).

**Distinguish prediction from explanation.** One of this book’s central

themes is the difference between predictive and explanatory modeling

(Chapters 9–11). Be ready to explain when you would prioritize

predictive accuracy versus causal interpretability, and what tradeoffs each

choice introduces. This distinction is interview gold for data science roles

because it signals analytical maturity.

**Discuss deployment-adjacent risks.** Training/serving skew, feedback

loops, and concept drift are central “Rules of ML” pitfalls that

experienced interviewers probe for (Zinkevich, n.d.). Even if you have

not deployed a model in production, demonstrating awareness of these

risks shows that you think beyond the notebook. AWS hiring managers

specifically note that candidates who discuss real-world failure modes

stand out (Amazon Web Services, 2024).

**Decision Implications to Practice Saying Out Loud**


“I would use stratified K-fold cross-validation here because the target

class is imbalanced, and a simple random split could produce folds with

very different class distributions.”

“The model has strong predictive accuracy, but I would not use it for

causal claims because the feature set includes post-treatment variables

that could introduce bias.”

“I would compare the cost of false positives against false negatives for

this business problem before choosing a classification threshold, rather

than defaulting to 0.5.”

For each business scenario, select the most appropriate primary evaluation

metric. These questions test your ability to match metrics to business

objectives—a critical interview skill.

1. A hospital builds a model to screen patients for a rare disease (2%

prevalence). Missing a positive case is life-threatening; false alarms

trigger a low-cost follow-up test. The best primary metric is:

(a) Accuracy

(b) Precision

(c) Recall

(d) F1

2. An e-commerce company builds a model to predict which products a

user will purchase next, displayed as “Recommended for You.”

Showing irrelevant products annoys users and reduces trust. The best

primary metric is:

(a) Recall

**Practice: Metric Selection Scenarios**


(b) Precision

(c) AUC

(d) Accuracy

3. A bank uses a model to rank loan applicants by default risk. Loan

officers review the top 100 riskiest applications manually. The best

primary metric is:

(a) Accuracy

(b) Recall

(c) AUC

(d) Mean Squared Error

4. A content moderation team builds a classifier to flag harmful posts.

Both missing harmful content and over-censoring legitimate posts are

costly. The best primary metric is:

(a) Precision

(b) Recall

(c) F1

(d) Accuracy

5. A model predicts house prices for a real estate website. Users see point

estimates. Predicted values that are systematically too high or too low

damage credibility. The best primary metric is:

(a) Accuracy

(b) R-squared or RMSE

(c) Precision

(d) AUC

6. A spam filter classifies incoming emails. Missing spam is annoying;

accidentally filtering a legitimate email could cause the user to miss an


important message. The best primary metric is:

(a) Recall — catch all spam

(b) Precision — never block legitimate email

(c) F1 or a threshold tuned to the user’s tolerance

(d) Accuracy

Answer Key

1. (c) Recall — cost of missing a positive case far outweighs cost of a follow-up

2. (b) Precision — irrelevant recommendations erode trust

3. (c) AUC — ranking quality matters when officers review a fixed number

4. (c) F1 — both error types are costly, so balance precision and recall

5. (b) R-squared or RMSE — regression problem, point estimate accuracy

6. (c) F1 or tuned threshold — both error types matter but with different costs; in practice,

tune the threshold


Feature selection questions test whether you understand the interaction

between feature engineering, model performance, and evaluation integrity.

These questions frequently appear in data science and ML engineering

interviews because incorrect feature selection is one of the most common

sources of silently inflated performance estimates.

**Explain why feature selection must be inside cross-validation**

**pipelines.** If selection uses the target variable (y), it must be performed

inside each fold so that selection refits only on training data. Performing

selection on the full dataset before splitting leaks information and

produces overly optimistic performance estimates. Scikit-learn’s

documentation on common pitfalls explicitly warns against this pattern

(Scikit-learn Developers, n.d.).

**Compare common feature selection tools at an interview level.** Be

ready to explain RFECV (a wrapper method that is systematic but

computationally expensive), permutation importance (model-agnostic

and computed on held-out data), and VIF (for multicollinearity in causal

models). Know when each is appropriate and what each measures (Scikit-

learn Developers, n.d.).

**Distinguish causal from predictive feature selection.** In predictive

work, features are selected to maximize out-of-sample performance. In

causal work, features are selected to produce valid coefficient estimates

—which sometimes means _including_ variables that hurt predictive

accuracy but are necessary for causal identification. This distinction

## Feature Selection (Chapter 16)

**What You Should Be Ready to Do**


maps directly to the prediction-versus-explanation theme from the

Foreword and Chapters 9–11.

“If selection uses y, it belongs inside the pipeline so each fold refits

selection only on training data.”

“I would use permutation importance here because it is model-agnostic

and computed on the validation set, which avoids the leakage risk of

computing importance on training data.”

“For this causal analysis I am keeping the control variables even though

they reduce R-squared, because omitting them would introduce omitted

variable bias into the treatment effect estimate.”

For each scenario, identify the correct ordering of pipeline steps. These

questions test your understanding of why certain operations must precede

others.

1. You are building a pipeline that includes imputation, scaling, feature

selection using mutual information, and logistic regression. Which

ordering prevents data leakage?

(a) Scale → Impute → Select → Fit

(b) Split → Impute → Scale → Select → Fit (all inside CV)

(c) Select → Impute → Scale → Fit

(d) Impute → Select → Split → Scale → Fit

2. A colleague one-hot encodes categorical variables and then applies

StandardScaler. Is this appropriate?

**Decision Implications to Practice Saying Out Loud**

**Practice: Pipeline Step Ordering**


(a) Yes — all features should be on the same scale

(b) No — one-hot encoded features are binary and scaling them

distorts their interpretation without improving most models

(c) No — you should never scale any features

(d) Yes — but only if the model is a decision tree

3. You need to impute missing values in a feature, then create an

interaction term between that feature and another. What is the correct

order?

(a) Create interaction first, then impute the result

(b) Impute first, then create the interaction — otherwise the

interaction inherits missing values

(c) It does not matter

(d) Drop rows with missing values instead of imputing

Answer Key

1. (b) All steps inside CV after splitting — feature selection uses the target, so it must be

refit per fold

2. (b) Scaling binary indicators distorts them without benefit for most algorithms

3. (b) Impute first — interaction terms propagate missingness

Deployment and monitoring questions are increasingly common in data

science interviews and are central to ML engineering and MLOps roles.

These questions test whether you understand that shipping a model is not the

finish line—ongoing monitoring and lifecycle management are part of

## Deployment, Monitoring, and Governance (Chapters

## 17–18)


responsible ML operations. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework

explicitly identifies monitoring, documentation, and accountability as core

components of trustworthy AI systems (NIST, 2023).

**Describe a realistic production loop.** Walk through the stages: logging

→ monitoring → drift detection → retraining triggers → governance

checks. Show that you understand models require ongoing maintenance,

not just initial deployment (Chapter 18).

**Explain common failure modes.** Distinguish between data drift (input

distribution changes), concept drift (the relationship between inputs and

outputs changes), and system failures (infrastructure, latency, data

pipeline breaks). Each requires a different detection and response strategy

(Chapter 18).

**Discuss model serialization and versioning.** Explain how you would

save a trained model (e.g., using joblib or pickle), version it with

metadata (training date, dataset hash, hyperparameters), and load it for

inference in a separate code path from training (Chapter 17).

**Reason about champion/challenger strategies.** Describe how you would

safely promote a new model: run it alongside the existing model,

compare performance on live data, and promote only when the challenger

demonstrably outperforms the champion under production conditions

(Chapter 18).

“I would separate training and inference code paths because they have

different dependencies, performance requirements, and failure modes.”

**What You Should Be Ready to Do**

**Decision Implications to Practice Saying Out Loud**


“Before retraining, I would check whether the performance drop is caused

by data drift or concept drift, because the response is different: data drift

may require upstream fixes, while concept drift may require model

architecture changes.”

“I would implement a monitoring dashboard that tracks PSI for input

features and key performance metrics across retraining cycles, with

alerting thresholds that trigger investigation before the model silently

degrades.”

Test your understanding of deployment and monitoring concepts. Select the

best answer for each scenario.

1. A model’s accuracy drops from 0.91 to 0.84 over three months in

production. Feature distributions look unchanged. What is the most

likely cause?

(a) Data drift

(b) Concept drift

(c) Training bug

(d) Overfitting during training

2. You deploy a fraud detection model. The operations team reports that

latency spikes cause 5% of transactions to time out before receiving a

prediction. What is the best immediate mitigation?

(a) Retrain the model on more data

(b) Set a default prediction for timed-out requests and investigate

the latency bottleneck

(c) Remove features to make the model simpler

**Practice: Deployment and Monitoring Scenarios**


(d) Switch to a rule-based system permanently

3. A feature used in training is computed from a table that is updated with

a 48-hour lag in production. What type of problem does this create?

(a) Label leakage

(b) Training–serving skew

(c) Class imbalance

(d) Multicollinearity

4. You want to detect when your model’s input distribution shifts. Which

metric is most appropriate?

(a) ROC AUC

(b) Population Stability Index (PSI)

(c) Mean Squared Error

(d) R-squared

5. After retraining a recommendation model, click-through rate drops

12%. The model metrics on the holdout set look fine. What is the most

likely explanation?

(a) The holdout set is too small

(b) A feedback loop: the model is now recommending items that it

previously promoted, creating a self-reinforcing bias in the training

data

(c) The learning rate was too high

(d) The model is underfitting

Answer Key

1. (b) Concept drift — features unchanged but the relationship between features and target

has shifted


2. (b) Default prediction plus latency investigation — never leave transactions without a

response

3. (b) Training–serving skew — the feature value at prediction time differs from what was

available during training

4. (b) PSI — designed specifically to measure distribution shift

5. (b) Feedback loop — offline metrics can look fine while online performance degrades

due to self-reinforcing training data

## 28.6Take-Home Assignments and Case Presentations

Many analytics and data science roles use take-home assignments as part of

the interview process. According to Interview Query’s analysis of over

10,000 data science interview experiences, approximately 25% of data

science interviews include a take-home assignment (Interview Query, 2025).

Unlike live coding, take-homes test a different set of skills: time

management, documentation quality, analytical judgment, and the ability to

deliver a complete, polished product under a deadline. Preparing for take-

homes requires a distinct strategy.

Interviewers evaluating take-home assignments typically assess four

dimensions. Glassdoor’s interview preparation guides confirm that these

criteria are consistent across companies, with reviewers typically spending

only a few minutes on initial assessment before deciding whether to dig

deeper (Glassdoor, 2024):

1. **Analytical approach.** Did you frame the problem clearly, make

reasonable assumptions, and choose appropriate methods? Interviewers

care more about your reasoning than about achieving the highest

possible accuracy.

**How Take-Homes Are Evaluated**


2. **Code quality.** Is the code clean, readable, and reproducible? Can

someone else run your notebook or script without errors? Include

comments that explain _why_ you made key decisions, not just what the

code does.

3. **Communication.** Is the written summary clear and concise? Does it

address the business question directly? Avoid burying the conclusion in

technical details—lead with the answer and then support it with

evidence.

4. **Judgment and completeness.** Did you handle edge cases,

acknowledge limitations, and avoid obvious pitfalls (like leakage or

overfitting)? Did you deliver a complete product within the time

constraint, or did you over-engineer one component and leave others

unfinished?

**Time-box aggressively.** Most take-homes specify a time limit (e.g., 4–8

hours). Allocate your time before you start: roughly 20% on exploration,

30% on modeling, 20% on evaluation, and 30% on write-up and polish. A

complete, well-documented 80% solution outscores a brilliant but

incomplete 50% solution every time. Indeed’s career guide reinforces

this, advising candidates to prioritize deliverable completeness over

technical sophistication (Indeed, 2024).

**Start with a baseline.** Build a simple model first (e.g., logistic regression

or a basic decision tree), evaluate it, and document the results. Then

iterate. This ensures you always have a complete submission, even if time

runs short.

**Write the summary first.** Draft your executive summary or introduction

before you start coding. This forces you to clarify the problem and your

**Preparation Tips**


approach upfront, and it ensures the write-up does not get rushed at the

end.

**Test reproducibility.** Before submitting, restart your kernel and run the

entire notebook from top to bottom. Nothing undermines a take-home

faster than code that does not execute. Include a requirements file or

environment specification if the assignment uses external packages.

**Show pipeline thinking.** Structure your submission to reflect the CRISP-

DM pipeline: problem understanding, data exploration, preparation,

modeling, evaluation, and conclusions. This mirrors the end-to-end

approach taught throughout this book (Chapter 1) and demonstrates

professional maturity.

**Download:** interview_prep_capstone_assignment.ipynb

This Jupyter notebook simulates a realistic take-home interview assignment

using the IBM HR Employee Attrition dataset (1,470 employees, 35

features). The assignment has five parts that mirror a real take-home: (A)

data exploration and profiling with specific numeric checkpoints, (B) SQL

queries against a SQLite database for department-level analysis, (C) building

and cross-validating a baseline classification pipeline, (D) identifying and

fixing a data leakage bug involving feature selection before splitting, and (E)

writing a 150–250 word executive summary for a non-technical audience.

Each part includes automated verification checks. Treat this as timed

practice: set a 3-hour limit to simulate real take-home conditions.

**Hands-On Practice: Capstone Take-Home Simulation**

**Case Presentations**


Some companies, particularly for senior or research-oriented roles, ask

candidates to present a past project or solve a case study in front of a panel.

Microsoft’s interview guidance notes that they evaluate both technical depth

and the ability to learn and adapt, looking for candidates who demonstrate a

“growth mindset” when discussing past work and challenges (Microsoft

Careers, 2024). The key to a strong case presentation is structure: state the

problem, describe your approach, present the results, and discuss limitations

and next steps. Practice delivering your presentation in 15–20 minutes (a

common time limit) and prepare for 10–15 minutes of questions. Anticipate

“what if” questions: What if the data were different? What if you had more

time? What would you change?

## 28.7Question Banks

The following question packs provide graduated practice across the major

interview topics. Each pack contains questions at three difficulty levels:

_warm-up_ (foundational recall), _standard_ (applied problem-solving), and

_stretch_ (tradeoff reasoning and system thinking). Use these in your deliberate

practice sessions: work through 3–5 questions per session, explain your

answers aloud, and identify gaps for targeted follow-up. For additional

practice beyond the questions below, comprehensive question banks are

available from Exponent (2026) and Analytics Vidhya (2026), both of which

provide hundreds of role-specific questions with solution guidance.

**Warm-up:**

**Practice Sets by Topic**

## Pack 1: SQL and Metrics (Analyst / DS)


1. Write a SQL query to find the top 10 customers by total revenue in the

last 12 months.

2. Explain the difference between INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, and FULL

OUTER JOIN. When would you use each?

3. What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING in SQL? Give an

example of when you need HAVING.

**Standard:**

1. Write a SQL query using a window function to calculate the 7-day

rolling average of daily active users.

2. A product manager asks you to define a “healthy” user for a

subscription product. What metrics would you propose, and how would

you validate that your definition is meaningful?

3. You notice a 15% drop in a key product metric week-over-week. Walk

through your investigation process: what would you check first,

second, and third?

**Stretch:**

1. Design a SQL query to identify users who churned (no activity in 30

days) but were highly active in the 30 days prior. How would you

present this to a product team to prioritize retention interventions?

2. A stakeholder wants to measure “engagement.” Propose three different

operationalizations of engagement, explain the tradeoffs of each, and

recommend one with justification.

**Download:** interview_prep_sql_practice.ipynb

**Hands-On Practice: SQL Notebook**


This Jupyter notebook creates an in-memory SQLite database with three

related tables (employees, departments, quarterly_sales) and provides 8 SQL

challenges at graduated difficulty. Challenges cover SELECT/WHERE,

GROUP BY, INNER JOIN, multi-table JOINs with aggregation, subqueries,

CASE WHEN, HAVING, and window functions (RANK). Each challenge

includes automated assert-based validation so you can check your answer

immediately. An answer key is provided at the bottom for instructor

reference.

For each SQL query and table description, predict the output without running

the query. These questions test your ability to mentally execute SQL—a skill

frequently assessed in live coding interviews.

**Schema:** Table _orders_ has columns: order_id (INT), customer_id (INT),

amount (DECIMAL), order_date (DATE), status (VARCHAR: ‘completed’,

‘pending’, ‘cancelled’). The table contains: (1, 101, 250, ‘2024-01-15’,

‘completed’), (2, 102, 150, ‘2024-01-20’, ‘completed’), (3, 101, 300, ‘2024-

02-10’, ‘pending’), (4, 103, 450, ‘2024-02-15’, ‘completed’), (5, 101, 100,

‘2024-03-01’, ‘cancelled’), (6, 102, 200, ‘2024-03-10’, ‘completed’).

1. _Query:_ SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status =

‘completed’ AND amount > 200; — How many rows are returned, and

what is the value?

(a) 1 row: count = 1

(b) 1 row: count = 2

(c) 1 row: count = 3

(d) 1 row: count = 4

**Practice: SQL Output Prediction**


2. _Query:_ SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders

GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(amount) > 300; — Which

customers appear?

(a) Only customer 101 (total = 650)

(b) Customers 101 (650) and 103 (450)

(c) Customers 101 (650), 102 (350), and 103 (450)

(d) All three customers

3. _Query:_ SELECT customer_id, amount, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY

amount DESC) as rnk FROM orders WHERE status = ‘completed’; —

What is the rank of customer 102’s $200 order?

(a) 2

(b) 3

(c) 4

(d) It is not included

4. _Query:_ SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) FROM orders

WHERE order_date >= ‘2024-02-01’; — What is the result?

(a) 2

(b) 3

(c) 4

(d) 6

5. _Query:_ SELECT status, AVG(amount) AS avg_amt FROM orders

GROUP BY status ORDER BY avg_amt DESC; — Which status has

the highest average?

(a) completed (avg = 262.50)

(b) pending (avg = 300.00)

(c) cancelled (avg = 100.00)


(d) completed (avg = 350.00)

6. _Query:_ SELECT customer_id FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id

HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2 AND SUM(CASE WHEN status =

‘cancelled’ THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0; — Which customer(s) appear?

(a) 101 only

(b) 102 only

(c) 101 and 102

(d) No customers

Answer Key

1. (b) count = 2 — orders 1 ($250, completed) and 4 ($450, completed) both meet status =

completed AND amount > 200

2. (c) All three: 101 = 250+300+100 = 650, 102 = 150+200 = 350, 103 = 450 — all exceed

300

```
3. (b) Rank 3 — completed orders by amount desc: 450, 250, 200, 150 — the $200 order
ranks 3rd
```
4. (b) 3 — distinct customers with orders on or after Feb 1: 101, 102, 103

5. (b) pending — only one pending order at $300, so avg = 300. Completed:

(250+150+450+200)/4 = 262.50. Cancelled: 100

6. (b) 102 only — customer 102 has 2 orders and 0 cancellations. Customer 101 has 3

orders but 1 cancellation

**Gold answer rubric:** Strong answers demonstrate SQL syntax correctness,

awareness of query performance (e.g., avoiding unnecessary subqueries), and

the ability to connect metrics to business decisions. Meta’s interview process

is particularly known for evaluating “product sense”—the ability to reason

about metrics, propose measurements, and investigate metric changes in

product contexts (Meta Careers, 2024). The best answers show judgment

about metric selection and acknowledge limitations of any single measure.


**Warm-up:**

1. Explain the bias-variance tradeoff. How does increasing model

complexity affect each?

2. What is the difference between L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge)

regularization? When would you prefer one over the other?

3. Describe the difference between bagging and boosting. Give an

example algorithm for each.

**Standard:**

1. You are building a model to predict whether a loan applicant will

default. Your dataset is highly imbalanced (5% defaults). What

evaluation metrics would you use, and why? How would you handle the

class imbalance?

2. Explain cross-validation. Why is it preferable to a single train/test split

for model evaluation? When might you use something other than

standard K-fold?

3. A colleague shows you a model with 99% training accuracy and 72%

test accuracy. What is likely happening, and what steps would you take

to address it?

**Stretch:**

1. You have built a gradient boosting model that achieves strong cross-

validated performance. A stakeholder asks you whether increasing

advertising spend _causes_ higher sales. Can you use this model to

answer that question? Why or why not? What would you need instead?

## Pack 2: Modeling and Evaluation (DS)


2. Describe a situation where a model with lower accuracy would be

preferred over one with higher accuracy. What factors beyond accuracy

should influence model selection in a production context?

**Gold answer rubric:** Strong answers show clear understanding of algorithm

mechanics, rigorous evaluation reasoning, and awareness of the prediction-

versus-explanation distinction. Scikit-learn’s documentation on cross-

validation provides an excellent reference for understanding proper

evaluation methodology and common mistakes to avoid (Scikit-learn

Developers, n.d.). The best answers connect modeling choices to business

context and acknowledge downstream operational implications.

**Warm-up:**

1. What is data leakage in machine learning? Give a concrete example.

2. What is the difference between filter methods and wrapper methods for

feature selection?

**Standard:**

1. You perform feature selection on the full dataset, then split into

train/test and evaluate. Your colleague says this is a problem. Explain

why, and describe how you would fix it.

2. Compare RFECV and permutation importance. When would you

choose each? What are the computational tradeoffs?

3. You are building a model to predict hospital readmissions. One feature

is “number of post-discharge follow-up calls.” Should you include it?

Why or why not?

## Pack 3: Feature Selection and Leakage (DS / ML)


**Stretch:**

1. Design a feature selection pipeline in scikit-learn that avoids leakage,

handles both numeric and categorical features, and integrates with

cross-validation. Describe the pipeline components and their order.

2. For a causal regression model, you have high VIF among several

control variables. A colleague suggests removing them to improve

model stability. Under what circumstances would this be appropriate,

and when would it be harmful to the analysis?

**Gold answer rubric:** Strong answers demonstrate understanding of why

leakage occurs (information from the future or the test set influencing

training), practical knowledge of selection tools, and judgment about when

selection serves prediction versus explanation. The best answers can design a

leak-free pipeline and articulate the reasoning to a non-technical stakeholder.

Scikit-learn’s RFECV and permutation importance documentation provides

detailed guidance on implementing these methods correctly within cross-

validation frameworks (Scikit-learn Developers, n.d.).

**Warm-up:**

1. What is the difference between batch inference and real-time

inference? Give an example use case for each.

2. Explain what model serialization is and why it matters for deployment.

**Standard:**

1. Describe the difference between data drift and concept drift. How

would you detect each in a production system?

## Pack 4: ML System Design (ML Eng / MLOps)


2. You have deployed a classification model that performs well initially

but degrades over three months. Walk through your debugging process.

What would you check and in what order?

3. Explain what a champion/challenger deployment strategy is and why it

is preferable to simply replacing the existing model.

**Stretch:**

1. Design an end-to-end ML system for a ride-sharing company that

predicts trip demand by geographic area for the next 24 hours. Include

data sources, feature engineering, model training, deployment, and

monitoring. Discuss how you would handle retraining and drift

detection.

2. A production model uses a feature derived from a third-party API that

occasionally returns null values. In training, these rows were dropped.

In production, you cannot drop predictions. How would you handle

this? What are the risks of each approach?

**Gold answer rubric:** Strong answers demonstrate awareness of the full

production lifecycle (not just training), practical knowledge of failure modes

and mitigation strategies, and the ability to reason about system-level

tradeoffs. The best answers reference real operational patterns and show

familiarity with monitoring, governance, and reliability concerns as

described in Chapters 17–18 and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework

(NIST, 2023).

## Pack 5: Behavioral Questions with STAR Prompts

## (All Tracks)


The following behavioral questions are organized by theme. These questions

reflect the types of behavioral probes used across major employers.

Amazon’s interview process, for example, maps every behavioral question to

one or more of their 16 Leadership Principles, with interviewers scoring

candidates against specific rubrics for each principle (Amazon, 2024). For

each question, practice constructing a complete STAR response (60–90

seconds) using examples from your coursework, projects, or work

experience.

**Teamwork and Collaboration:**

1. Tell me about a time you worked on a data project with a team. What

was your specific contribution, and how did you handle disagreements

about approach?

2. Describe a situation where you had to explain a technical finding to a

non-technical stakeholder. How did you adapt your communication?

**Ambiguity and Initiative:**

1. Tell me about a time you started a project without clear requirements.

How did you define the scope and move forward?

2. Describe a situation where you identified a problem or opportunity that

no one else had noticed. What did you do about it?

**Failure and Learning:**

1. Tell me about a time a model or analysis produced unexpected or

incorrect results. How did you discover the problem, and what did you

do?

2. Describe a project that did not go as planned. What would you do

differently if you could start over?


**Technical Leadership and Judgment:**

1. Tell me about a time you had to choose between two reasonable

analytical approaches. How did you make the decision, and what was

the outcome?

2. Describe a situation where you had to push back on a request because

the data did not support the conclusion someone wanted. How did you

handle it?

**Gold answer rubric:** Strong behavioral answers follow the STAR structure

completely (no missing components), quantify results where possible, clarify

the candidate’s personal role versus the team’s work, and demonstrate

reflection or learning. The best answers connect the experience to a broader

principle or habit that the candidate applies consistently.

(b) Amazon

(c) Meta

(d) Microsoft

(a) Always use all available features

(b) Perform feature selection inside the cross-validation pipeline so

each fold refits selection on training data only

(b) Include a clear README, explain assumptions, and annotate

(c) 6–8

(b) Check for data quality issues and external events before

2. (b) Deliberate practice

3. (b) Task

4. (c) Data Analyst / BI

8. (b) Differences between training and production data/transforms

16. (d) Compare to peers

17. (b) Check data quality and external events first

18. (b) More predictive, reduce bias

19. (b) 15–20 min presentation, 10–15 min Q&A

20. (c) 36%


## 28.9Study Plans

The study plans below provide structured, day-by-day schedules built around

the deliberate practice principles from Section 3. Each day focuses on a

single topic and includes a specific activity from this chapter or a referenced

book chapter. Sessions are designed to be 60–90 minutes each—short enough

to maintain focus and long enough to build real skill.

Choose the plan that fits your timeline. Supplement your practice with free

tools: Google’s Interview Warmup provides AI feedback on spoken answers

(Google, 2025), Coursera offers role-specific interview preparation courses

(Coursera, 2025), and platforms like Interview Query and Kaggle provide

real take-home challenges from actual company interviews (Interview Query,

2025; Kaggle, 2024). If you have more than four weeks, repeat the 4-week

plan with different questions from the question banks each cycle.

Use this plan when you have an interview scheduled within two weeks. This

plan prioritizes the highest-frequency topics and builds one complete STAR

story per day.
